metadata.title: Guideline on the investigation of drug interactions

rules.rule_text: The potential for pharmacokinetic interactions between new medicinal products and already marketed drugs must be evaluated, covering both effects of the medicinal product on other drugs and the effect of other drugs on the medicinal product. The effect of concomitant food intake must also be investigated.
rules.source_section: Executive Summary
rules.verbatim_quote: The potential for pharmacokinetic interactions between new medicinal products and already marketed drugs should be evaluated. This applies to both effects of the medicinal product on other drugs as well as the effect of other drugs on the medicinal product. Furthermore the effect of concomitant food intake needs to be investigated.
rules.exceptions: Alternative approaches acceptable if adequately justified and driven by science and the expected clinical consequence of the interaction.

rules.rule_text: Pharmacokinetic interaction studies must generally be performed in humans. In vitro studies must be performed using human enzymes and transporters. Deviations from this approach must be well justified and supported by scientific literature.
rules.source_section: 5. Pharmacokinetic interactions
rules.verbatim_quote: Pharmacokinetic interaction studies should generally be performed in humans. Preclinical studies in animals may sometimes be relevant, but due to the marked species differences, direct extrapolation of such results to humans is difficult. Therefore, the wording in vivo below means in humans. Similarly in vitro studies should be performed using human enzymes and transporters. Deviations from this approach should be well justified and supported by scientific literature.
rules.exceptions: Preclinical animal studies may sometimes be relevant but results cannot be directly extrapolated to humans.

rules.rule_text: The effect of food intake on the rate and extent of absorption of an orally administered investigational drug must be investigated as early as possible during drug development. The effect of a high-fat meal must be investigated as worst-case scenario.
rules.source_section: 5.1 Effects of food intake on the pharmacokinetics of the investigational drug
rules.verbatim_quote: The effect of food intake on the rate and extent of absorption of an orally administered investigational drug should be investigated as early as possible during drug development to optimize dose finding and to ensure optimal food recommendations in the phase III clinical studies and drug labelling. In general, recommendations regarding timing of drug intake in relation to food should aim at minimising variability and obtaining optimal exposure. The effect of a high-fat meal on the absorption of the investigational drug should be investigated as worst-case scenario.
rules.exceptions: If formulation is modified during clinical development, additional food interaction studies may be needed. Recommendations may differ for nonlinear pharmacokinetics.

rules.rule_text: The standardised food interaction study must use a high-fat meal containing 800-1000 kcal with 500-600 kcal from fat, administered after a 10-hour fasting period, with the drug administered 30 minutes after start of the meal with 240 ml of water.
rules.source_section: Appendix I — Standard procedures for food interaction studies
rules.verbatim_quote: A single-dose of the investigational drug is administered with 240 ml of water after a 10-hour fasting period and 30 minutes after intake of a meal has been started. The high fat meal should contain 800-1000 kcal with 500-600 kcal from fat and 250 kcal from carbohydrates.
rules.exceptions: Substitutions in the test meal are acceptable as long as the meal provides similar amounts of calories from protein, carbohydrate, and fat, and has comparable meal volume and viscosity. A moderate meal (400-500 kcal with fat contributing ca. 150 kcal) may also be used where relevant.

rules.rule_text: In vitro information supporting the prediction of the effects of other medicinal products on the pharmacokinetics of the investigational drug must preferably be available before introducing the investigational product to patients (phase II) and is generally required before starting phase III.
rules.source_section: 5.2 Effects of other medicinal products on the pharmacokinetics of the investigational drug
rules.verbatim_quote: In vitro information supporting the prediction of the effects by other medicinal products on the pharmacokinetics of the investigational drug should preferably be available before introducing the investigational product to patients (phase II) and is generally required before starting phase III.
rules.exceptions: Case-by-case determination based on the possibility of excluding potentially interacting medicines, pharmacokinetic characteristics, and safety of the drug at higher exposures.

rules.rule_text: If the solubility or dissolution of the drug is markedly pH dependent in the physiological pH range, the potential effect of drugs which increase gastric pH (e.g. proton pump inhibitors, H2-receptor antagonists or antacids) must be investigated in vivo.
rules.source_section: 5.2.1 Absorption — A. Interactions affecting solubility
rules.verbatim_quote: If the solubility of the drug or the dissolution of the formulation is markedly pH dependent in the physiological pH range, the potential effect of drugs which increase gastric pH, such as proton pump inhibitors, H2-receptor antagonists or antacids, should be investigated in vivo.
rules.exceptions: If indicated by physicochemical properties, potential for complex binding may need to be investigated in vitro and an in vivo study could be considered.

rules.rule_text: Involvement of transporters in drug absorption must be evaluated in vitro in Caco-2 cells. When a candidate transporter has been identified and interactions through inhibition are likely to be clinically relevant, an in vivo study with a strong inhibitor is recommended if known inhibitors are registered as medicinal products in the EU.
rules.source_section: 5.2.1 Absorption — B. Interactions affecting intestinal active transport
rules.verbatim_quote: It is recommended that the involvement of transporters in drug absorption is evaluated in vitro in Caco-2 cells. When a candidate transporter has been identified, and interactions through inhibition are likely to be clinically relevant, an in vivo study with a strong inhibitor is recommended if known inhibitors are registered as medicinal products in the EU.
rules.exceptions: In vivo studies in subjects of certain genotypes giving rise to markedly altered expression or activity of the transporter may be useful as an alternative.

rules.rule_text: In vitro metabolism studies must generally be performed before starting phase I to identify the main metabolites formed in vitro. These studies must identify candidate enzymes responsible for the main metabolic pathways of the parent drug.
rules.source_section: 5.2.3 Metabolism
rules.verbatim_quote: In vitro metabolism studies should generally be performed before starting phase I to identify the main metabolites formed in vitro. These studies provide information necessary for the extrapolation of preclinical safety data to man and also allow for early screening of main metabolites found in vitro for target pharmacological activity. In vitro studies should also be performed to identify candidate enzymes responsible for the main metabolic pathways of the parent drug.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: The mass-balance study results must generally be available before starting phase III. Enzymes involved in metabolic pathways estimated to contribute to 25% or more of drug elimination must be identified if possible and the in vivo contribution quantified.
rules.source_section: 5.2.3 Metabolism
rules.verbatim_quote: The results of the mass-balance study should generally be available before starting phase III. In general, enzymes involved in metabolic pathways estimated to contribute to ≥ 25% of drug elimination should be identified if possible and the in vivo contribution quantified. This applies to cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes and non-CYP enzymes.
rules.exceptions: Applies to both CYP and non-CYP enzymes.

rules.rule_text: If cytochrome P450 enzymes are identified as candidate enzymes in the main elimination pathways, evaluation of the pharmacokinetics with and without concomitant administration of a strong enzyme inhibitor is recommended to verify and quantify the involvement of a specific enzyme.
rules.source_section: 5.2.3 Metabolism — A) Interaction studies with inhibitors of cytochrome P450 enzymes
rules.verbatim_quote: If cytochrome P450 enzymes are identified as candidate enzymes involved in the main elimination pathways of the drug (or in major formation or elimination pathways of clinically relevant active metabolites), evaluation of the pharmacokinetics of the investigational drug with and without concomitant administration of a strong enzyme inhibitor (see Appendices IV and V) is recommended to verify and quantify the involvement of a specific enzyme in the investigational drug elimination.
rules.exceptions: If the interaction study with the strong inhibitor results in a marked effect, an additional study with a moderate inhibitor is recommended. PBPK simulations may substitute in specific circumstances.

rules.rule_text: If the drug is eliminated through metabolism mainly catalysed by one or more inducible enzymes, or if elimination is catalysed by CYP3A to a limited extent, an interaction study with a potent inducer is recommended.
rules.source_section: 5.2.3 Metabolism — C) Interaction studies with inducers
rules.verbatim_quote: If the drug is eliminated through metabolism mainly catalysed by one or more inducible enzymes, or if elimination is catalysed by CYP3A only to a limited extent, an interaction study with a potent inducer is recommended. This also applies to situations where it may not be excluded that enzyme induction will affect drug exposure to a clinically relevant extent, such as drugs mainly eliminated through secretion by inducible transport proteins.
rules.exceptions: An interaction study is not necessary if the effects are highly predictable (e.g. rifampicin on a drug mainly eliminated through CYP3A catalysed metabolism) and likely to result in a contraindication.

rules.rule_text: The possible involvement of OATP1B1 and OATP1B3 uptake transport must be investigated in vitro for drugs estimated to have 25% or more hepatic elimination.
rules.source_section: 5.2.4 Active uptake and secretion in drug elimination
rules.verbatim_quote: Therefore, the possible involvement of OATP1B1 and 1B3 uptake transport should be investigated in vitro for drugs estimated to have ≥ 25% hepatic elimination (clearance by hepatic metabolism and biliary secretion together contributing to ≥ 25%).
rules.exceptions: As scientific knowledge evolves, other hepatic uptake transporters may need screening if their inhibition has generally been observed to lead to large effects on drug elimination.

rules.rule_text: If renal secretion or biliary/gut wall secretion separately is estimated to account for more than 25% of drug elimination, attempts must be made to identify the transporter(s) involved in the active secretion.
rules.source_section: 5.2.4 Active uptake and secretion in drug elimination
rules.verbatim_quote: In line with the requirements for enzyme identification, if renal secretion or biliary/gut wall secretion separately is estimated to account for more than 25% of drug elimination, attempts should be made to identify the transporter(s) involved in the active secretion.
rules.exceptions: Quantitative importance of biliary/gut wall secretion should be based on mass balance data supported by available interaction data, potential pharmacogenetic information, data in patients with hepatic impairment, Caco-2 cell permeability, etc.

rules.rule_text: When indicated, drug interactions relevant to special subpopulations (including carriers of certain alleles, patients with impaired renal function, and young paediatric patients under 2 years) must be investigated. An in vivo study exploring the interaction in the subpopulation is recommended when indicated.
rules.source_section: 5.2.5 Special populations
rules.verbatim_quote: An interaction effect may not be directly extrapolated to specific subpopulations that have a markedly different contribution of the affected enzyme and/or transporter to the clearance of the investigational drug. Such subpopulations may include carriers of certain alleles coding for an enzyme or transporter subject to genetic polymorphisms, patients with impaired renal function and young paediatric patients (< 2 years). When indicated, drug interactions relevant to the subpopulation should be investigated. An in vivo study exploring the interaction in the subpopulation is recommended.
rules.exceptions: If a clinical study is not possible, a worst case estimation of the effect on the active substance exposure may be performed. PBPK simulations may also be acceptable if adequately qualified.

rules.rule_text: In vitro data on the effects of the investigational parent drug on the pharmacokinetics of other drugs must preferably be available before starting phase II studies, and must be available before starting phase III.
rules.source_section: 5.3 Effects of the investigational drug on the pharmacokinetics of other drugs
rules.verbatim_quote: In vitro data on the effects of the investigational parent drug on the pharmacokinetics of other drugs should preferably be available before starting phase II studies unless all concomitant drug treatments at risk of being clinically relevantly affected can be avoided in these studies. The in vitro information should be available before starting phase III.
rules.exceptions: Exception if all concomitant drug treatments at risk of being clinically relevantly affected can be avoided in phase II studies.

rules.rule_text: In vitro studies must be performed to investigate whether the investigational drug inhibits the cytochrome P450 enzymes most commonly involved in drug metabolism, presently including CYP1A2, CYP2B6, CYP2C8, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2D6, and CYP3A. CYP3A4 inhibition must be investigated using both midazolam and testosterone as substrates.
rules.source_section: 5.3.3.1 Enzyme inhibition — A. In vitro enzyme inhibition studies
rules.verbatim_quote: In vitro studies should be performed to investigate whether the investigational drug inhibits the cytochrome P450 enzymes most commonly involved in drug metabolism. These presently include CYP1A2, CYP2B6, CYP2C8, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2D6, and CYP3A. CYP3A4 inhibition should be investigated using both midazolam and testosterone as substrates.
rules.exceptions: In addition, inhibition of UGTs known to be involved in drug interactions (including UGT1A1 and UGT2B7) must be studied if one of the major elimination pathways of the investigational drug is direct glucuronidation.

rules.rule_text: The in vitro enzyme inhibition study must include an investigation of whether pre-incubation with the investigational drug alters the inhibitory potential. If pre-incubation affects potency, time-dependent inhibition (TDI) is present and more detailed investigations including determination of kinact and KI are required.
rules.source_section: 5.3.3.1 Enzyme inhibition — A. In vitro enzyme inhibition studies
rules.verbatim_quote: The study should include an investigation of whether pre-incubation with the investigational drug alters the inhibitory potential of the drug. If the pre-incubation affects the potency, more detailed investigations are needed. If the inhibition is enhanced by pre-incubations, time-dependent inhibition (TDI) is present. For mechanism based inactivators, kinact (maximum inactivation rate constant) and KI (the inhibitor concentration producing half the maximal rate of inactivation) should be determined.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: For in vitro enzyme inhibition studies, the concentration range must be sufficient for determining Ki. For orally administered drugs with pronounced intestinal expression (e.g. CYP3A4), the concentration range must be sufficient for determining Ki at or below 0.1-fold the maximum expected dose taken at one occasion per 250 ml. For hepatic enzymes, Ki must be determinable at or below 50-fold the mean unbound Cmax.
rules.source_section: 5.3.3.1 Enzyme inhibition — Intestinal exposure / Hepatic (and renal) exposure
rules.verbatim_quote: If the drug is orally administered and the enzyme studied has pronounced intestinal expression (e.g. CYP3A4) the concentration range should be sufficient for determining a Ki ≤ 0.1-fold the maximum expected dose taken at one occasion /250 ml. If the enzyme studied is mainly available in the liver, or the kidney/another organ with main drug input from the systemic circulation, the concentration range should allow determination of a Ki which is ≤ 50-fold the mean unbound Cmax obtained during treatment with the highest dose.
rules.exceptions: If solubility limitations in intestinal fluid exist, lower concentrations may be sufficient. When estimating fu, figures lower than 1% should not be used due to uncertainties in the estimation.

rules.rule_text: For the basic model assessment of reversible enzyme inhibition in vivo risk: for orally administered drugs where the enzyme has marked abundance in the enterocyte (e.g. CYP3A), an in vivo interaction study is recommended if [I]/Ki >= 10, where [I] is the maximum dose taken at one occasion per 250 ml. For hepatic and other systemic enzymes, an in vivo study is recommended if [I]/Ki >= 0.02, where [I] is the unbound mean Cmax at the highest recommended dose.
rules.source_section: 5.3.3.1 Enzyme inhibition — A:1 Basic Model — I) Reversible inhibition
rules.verbatim_quote: For orally administered drugs if the enzyme has marked abundance in the enterocyte (e.g. CYP3A): [I] / Ki ≥ 10 where [I] is the maximum dose taken at one occasion/250 ml. For drugs regardless of mode of administration and inhibition of enzymes in the liver, or in organs, exposed to the drug through the systemic circulation [I] / Ki ≥ 0.02 where [I] is the unbound mean Cmax obtained during treatment with the highest recommended dose
rules.exceptions: The lowest figure on free fraction recommended is 1% due to the uncertainties in the estimation. If Ki has been determined in several in vitro systems or with several substrates for the same enzyme, the lowest Ki adequately measured should be used.

rules.rule_text: For mechanism-based inactivation (MBI), if R >= 1.25 (i.e. >= 20% inhibition) using the basic model formula with the appropriate drug concentrations, in vivo inhibition may not be excluded and a multiple-dose in vivo interaction study is recommended.
rules.source_section: 5.3.3.1 Enzyme inhibition — A:1 Basic Model — II) Mechanism based inactivation
rules.verbatim_quote: If ≥ 20% inhibition is obtained, i.e., R ≥1.25, using the drug concentrations presented above, in vivo inhibition may not be excluded and a multiple dose in vivo interaction study is recommended (see Sections 5.4.3 and 5.4.4).
rules.exceptions: The mechanistic static model (A:2) may be used as an alternative to assess MBI. PBPK dynamic models may also be used if adequately qualified.

rules.rule_text: If the PBPK dynamic model predicts an inhibition of more than 20% of the clearance of the probe substrate (AUCR > 1.25), a significant interaction in vivo cannot be excluded and it is recommended to perform an in vivo study.
rules.source_section: 5.3.3.1 Enzyme inhibition — A:3 Dynamic PBPK model
rules.verbatim_quote: If the simulation predicts an inhibition of > 20% of the clearance of the probe substrate, a significant interaction in vivo cannot be excluded and it is recommended to perform an in vivo study. If the results of the simulation with appropriate sensitivity analyses are negative and the modelling is acceptable, no in vivo study of the effect of the specific enzyme is required.
rules.exceptions: If sensitivity analyses are negative and modelling is acceptable, no in vivo study of the effect of the specific enzyme is required, regardless of the results of the basic model or static mechanistic model.

rules.rule_text: Studies must be performed to investigate whether the investigational drug induces enzymes and transporters via activation of nuclear receptors. This must initially be investigated in vitro using cultured (fresh or cryopreserved) hepatocytes from at least 3 different evaluable donors, followed by in vivo studies if indicated by the in vitro results.
rules.source_section: 5.3.3.2 Enzyme induction and down-regulation — A. Enzyme induction — in vitro studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Studies should be performed to investigate whether the investigational drug induces enzymes and transporters via activation of nuclear receptors, the Ah-receptor or, if relevant, other drug regulation pathways. Usually, this is initially investigated in vitro followed by in vivo studies if indicated by the in vitro results. Cultured hepatocytes (fresh or cryopreserved) are the preferred in vitro system for induction (and down-regulation) in vitro studies.
rules.exceptions: It is also possible to investigate induction directly in vivo. Minimally derived hepatocyte lines (e.g. HepaRG), nuclear receptor binding assays, or reporter gene assays are considered as supportive data only.

rules.rule_text: A potential human teratogen must be studied in vivo for effects on contraceptive steroids if the drug is intended for use in fertile women, regardless of the in vitro induction study results.
rules.source_section: 5.3.3.2 Enzyme induction and down-regulation — A. Enzyme induction — in vitro studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Therefore, a potential human teratogen (Definition given in EMEA/CHMP/203927/2005) needs to be studied in vivo for effects on contraceptive steroids if the drug is intended for use in fertile women, regardless of the in vitro induction study results.
rules.exceptions: None — this requirement is absolute regardless of in vitro results.

rules.rule_text: For the basic in vitro induction method, the in vitro study is considered positive for enzyme induction if incubations at the applicable concentration give rise to more than 100% increase in mRNA, and the increase is concentration dependent. An observed increase of less than 100% can be considered negative only when the increase in mRNA is less than 20% of the response of the positive control.
rules.source_section: 5.3.3.2 Enzyme induction — A:1 Basic method
rules.verbatim_quote: The in vitro study is considered positive for enzyme induction if incubations with the investigational drug at the concentration given above give rise to a more than 100% increase in mRNA and the increase is concentration dependent. To ensure adequate sensitivity of the assay, an observed concentration-dependent increase in mRNA of <100% can be considered as a negative finding only when the increase in mRNA is less than 20% of the response of the positive control (rifampicin 20 μM or, for Ah-receptor activation, omeprazole 50 μM).
rules.exceptions: A positive or inconclusive in vitro result should be confirmed in vivo or lack of induction potential needs to be shown in another in vitro study. If a 50% decrease in mRNA is observed which may not be attributable to cell toxicity, down-regulation is suspected and an in vivo study is recommended.

rules.rule_text: In vitro inhibition studies are recommended to investigate whether the investigational drug inhibits any of the transporters known to be involved in clinically relevant in vivo drug interactions. These presently include P-glycoprotein/MDR1 (ABCB1), OATP1B1 (SLCO1B1), OATP1B3 (SLCO1B3), OCT2 (SLC22A2), OAT1 (SLC22A6), OAT3 (SLC22A8), and BCRP (ABCG2). In vitro data on transporter inhibition must preferably be available before initiating phase III.
rules.source_section: 5.3.4.1 Inhibition of transport proteins
rules.verbatim_quote: In vitro inhibition studies are recommended to investigate whether the investigational drug inhibits any of the transporters known to be involved in clinically relevant in vivo drug interactions. Presently, these include P-glycoprotein/MDR1 (ABCB1), OATP1B1 (SLCO1B1), OATP1B3 (SLCO1B3), OCT2 (SLC22A2), OAT1 (SLC22A6), OAT3 (SLC22A8) and BCRP (ABCG2). In vitro data on transporter inhibition should preferably be available before initiating phase III.
rules.exceptions: OCT1 (SLC22A1), MATE1 (SLC47A1) and MATE2 (SLC47A2) could also be considered. Inhibition of BSEP (ABCB11) should also preferably be investigated; if in vitro BSEP inhibition is found, adequate biochemical monitoring including serum bile salts is recommended during drug development.

rules.rule_text: For transporter inhibition in vitro studies, in vivo inhibition at a given site can be excluded if the observed Ki value is higher than or equal to the defined concentration thresholds: 0.1*dose/250 ml for intestinal efflux transporters; 25*unbound hepatic inlet concentration for hepatic uptake transporters; 50*unbound Cmax for renal and hepatic efflux transporters.
rules.source_section: 5.3.4.1 Inhibition of transport proteins
rules.verbatim_quote: In vivo inhibition of a transporter at a certain site can be excluded if the observed Ki value is higher than or equal to the concentrations given above (0.1*dose/250 ml, 25*([I]u,inlet,max, or 50* unbound Cmaxu, respectively).
rules.exceptions: The unbound fraction should be set to 1% if determined to be less than 1%. Due to high inter-laboratory variability in Pgp inhibition parameter estimation, use of two separate systems is recommended for this transporter.

rules.rule_text: If an investigational drug has been observed to be an inducer of enzymes via nuclear receptors (PXR and CAR), a study investigating the in vivo induction of Pgp mediated transport is recommended. The need for in vivo studies of the potential inducing effect on other transporters regulated through the same pathways must also be considered.
rules.source_section: 5.3.4.2 Induction of transport proteins
rules.verbatim_quote: If an investigational drug has been observed to be an inducer of enzymes via nuclear receptors such as PXR and CAR, it is likely that transporters regulated through these receptors will be induced. If PXR and/or CAR mediated induction is observed in vivo, a study investigating the in vivo induction of Pgp mediated transport is recommended.
rules.exceptions: None stated.

rules.rule_text: In vivo interaction studies must generally use a cross-over or sequential design. Parallel group design is generally not recommended due to confounding inter-individual variability. Comparisons with historical controls are generally not acceptable.
rules.source_section: 5.4 Design of in vivo studies
rules.verbatim_quote: An in vivo interaction study usually is of cross-over or sequential design. Parallel group design is generally not recommended due to the confounding inter-individual variability. Comparisons with historical controls are generally not acceptable. However, if a crossover or sequential design is impossible to perform, a parallel group design may be acceptable.
rules.exceptions: If a crossover or sequential design is impossible to perform, a parallel group design may be acceptable.

rules.rule_text: In vivo studies performed to investigate whether the investigational drug inhibits or induces a drug metabolising enzyme or transporter must be performed with well-validated probe drugs. The probe drug for CYP3A must be midazolam (oral), and CYP3A4 inhibition must be investigated using both midazolam and testosterone as substrates in vitro.
rules.source_section: 5.4.2 Probe drugs and cocktail studies
rules.verbatim_quote: In vivo studies performed to investigate whether the investigational drug inhibits or induces a drug metabolising enzyme or transporter in vivo should be performed with well validated probe drugs. A probe drug is a drug which is exclusively or almost exclusively eliminated through metabolism catalysed by one specific enzyme or eliminated through excretion by one specific transporter in vivo. The probe drug for CYP3A should be subject to both marked intestinal and hepatic 3A catalysed metabolism. The use of orally administered midazolam is recommended.
rules.exceptions: Other probe drugs for various enzymes are given in Appendix VII. Only probe drugs for the most commonly involved CYPs are listed; others may be used if justified. Marker reactions may sometimes be used.

rules.rule_text: The systemic exposure of the perpetrator drug in an in vivo interaction study must be obtained with the highest generally recommended dose under therapeutic (steady state) conditions. The duration of treatment with the perpetrator drug must be long enough to cover at least 90% of the plasma concentration-time curve (sampling period) of the victim drug.
rules.source_section: 5.4.3 Dose, formulation and time of administration — A. The perpetrator drug
rules.verbatim_quote: The systemic exposure of the potential perpetrator drug should be the exposure obtained with the highest generally recommended dose under therapeutic (steady state) conditions. The duration of the treatment with the perpetrator drug should be long enough to certify that it covers at least 90% of the plasma concentration-time curve (sampling period) of the victim drug.
rules.exceptions: If the highest expected exposure is not studied, this should be well justified (e.g. safety aspects). PBPK simulations may be used to predict the likely effect if all interaction mechanisms are included and the model predicts the results of the studied dose adequately.

rules.rule_text: For time-dependent interactions (induction or time-dependent inhibition), the study must aim to investigate the interaction effect at the time-point where the induction or inhibition effect is at or near its maximum. If the interaction study will be used for dosing recommendations, a study investigating the true maximum effect is needed.
rules.source_section: 5.4.4 Time dependencies
rules.verbatim_quote: For time-dependent interactions, i.e. induction or 'time-dependent' inhibition, the study should aim to investigate the interaction effect at the time-point where the induction or inhibition effect is at or near its maximum. If the study aims to investigate whether an investigational drug is an inducer or a time-dependent inhibitor in vivo, determining 80% of the induction or inhibition effect is sufficient. If the interaction study will be used for dosing recommendations, a study investigating the true maximum effect is needed.
rules.exceptions: Determining 80% of the induction or inhibition effect is sufficient if the study aims only to confirm whether induction/inhibition occurs. The chosen duration should be justified, e.g. by simulations.

rules.rule_text: If there are active metabolites contributing to the efficacy and safety of the drug, their exposure must be evaluated in the interaction studies. Pharmacokinetic parameters determined must include Cmax, Tmax, AUC, CL, and the terminal half-life.
rules.source_section: 5.4.5 Active metabolites / 5.4.6 Pharmacokinetic parameters
rules.verbatim_quote: If there are active metabolites contributing to the efficacy and safety of the drug, the exposure to these metabolites should be evaluated in the interaction studies. The pharmacokinetic parameters determined should be the ones relevant for the use and interpretation of the study results. Usually such parameters include Cmax, Tmax and AUC, CL and the terminal half-life.
rules.exceptions: Pharmacologically active metabolites which do not contribute significantly to in vivo effects during normal conditions should also be considered if a marked increase in exposure from the interaction could be clinically relevant.

rules.rule_text: The consequences of an observed in vivo (or in vitro) interaction must be assessed and suitable treatment recommendations or warnings given in the SmPC. Drug interaction information must be presented in SmPC sections 4.5 and 5.2, cross-referred to sections 4.2, 4.3 or 4.4 if relevant.
rules.source_section: 5.7 Translation into treatment recommendations / 7. Inclusion of information and recommendations in the SmPC
rules.verbatim_quote: The consequences of an observed in vivo (or in vitro) interaction should be assessed and suitable treatment recommendations or warnings given. Information about drug interactions should be presented in the SmPC sections 4.5 and 5.2 (e.g. for food-interactions) and cross-referred to the sections 4.2, 4.3 or 4.4 if relevant. Section 4.5 should contain all detailed information on drug interactions and only the recommendation should be given in the cross-referred sections.
rules.exceptions: Vague wording such as 'caution is advised' should be avoided in favour of a recommendation on proposed actions.

rules.rule_text: Enzyme inhibitors and inducers must, if possible, be classified as mild, moderate or strong according to their in vivo potency. A strong inhibitor causes a greater than 5-fold increase in plasma AUC or >= 80% decrease in oral clearance. A moderate inhibitor causes a greater than 2-fold increase in AUC or 50-80% inhibition of oral clearance. A mild inhibitor causes a 1.25-2 fold increase in AUC or <= 50% inhibition of oral clearance.
rules.source_section: Appendix VIII — Classification of inhibitors and inducers according to potency
rules.verbatim_quote: A strong inhibitor causes a > 5 fold increase in the plasma AUC values or ≥ 80% decrease in oral clearance, a moderate inhibitor causes a > 2-fold increase in the plasma AUC or 50 - ≤ 80% inhibition of oral clearance, a mild inhibitor causes 1.25 to 2 fold increase in the plasma AUC or ≤ 50% inhibition of oral clearance. Inducers of CYP3A should be classified based on the effect on oral midazolam clearance or plasma AUC. A ≤ 50%, > 50 - ≤ 80% and > 80% reduction in midazolam AUC after oral administration classifies an investigational drug as mild, moderate and strong inducer, respectively.
rules.exceptions: Oral midazolam is recommended when classifying a drug as a CYP3A inhibitor due to the varying extent of intestinal first-pass metabolism. The potency classification must be presented in the SmPC together with the magnitude of interaction effect observed.

rules.rule_text: Information about interactions between medicinal products and herbal medicinal products or specific food products (e.g. grapefruit juice) must be investigated and translated into general recommendations regarding use. For new herbal preparations the potential for interactions must be investigated by the applicant. The SmPC must include clear recommendations regarding food intake effects on pharmacokinetics.
rules.source_section: 6. Herbal medicinal products and specific food products / 5.7.4 Food effects
rules.verbatim_quote: Information about interactions between medicinal products and herbal medicinal products or specific food products such as grapefruit juice is usually based on the scientific literature and translated into general recommendations regarding use of the food products or herbal products containing a specific component. For new herbal preparations the potential for interactions should be investigated by the applicant. If food interaction studies indicate that there should be specific recommendations on how to take the drug in relation to food, clear information about this should be given in the SmPC.
rules.exceptions: For traditional and well-established herbal preparations, the potential for interaction should be clarified only if reports point to clinically relevant interactions in humans. The interaction potential of one specific herbal product is difficult to extrapolate to other products produced from the same raw source material.
